name: Student Grade Book
scenario: Build a complete digital grade book for a university (think BITS Pilani or IIT's internal grade management). Students take multiple courses. Teachers need to: look up any student's grades by ID, find the class average per course, see which students are at risk (below passing threshold), and generate a grade distribution report. All powered by HashMap.
description: Grade book system using HashMap for student lookup, course grade storage, and statistical aggregation. Demonstrates all three HashMap patterns (lookup, counting/aggregation, grouping) in one cohesive educational domain.

core.description: `Student` class: studentId (String), name, Map<String, Integer> courseGrades (course name → grade). Constructor takes id and name.

Student: `addGrade(String course, int grade)`, `getGrade(String course)` returns Integer (null if not enrolled), `getGPA()` returns average of all grades, `isPassing(int threshold)` returns true if ALL grades >= threshold.
`GradeBook` class: `Map<String, Student>` by studentId. `enrollStudent(Student)`, `findStudent(String id)` returns Student or null.
`courseAverage(String course)` → double average grade for that course across all enrolled students who took it.
`atRisk(int threshold)` → List<Student> who are not passing (isPassing returns false). Sort by GPA ascending.
`gradeDistribution(String course)` → Map<String, Integer> counting how many students got each letter grade (A:90+, B:80+, C:70+, D:60+, F:below 60).
main() demonstrates all operations with at least 5 students and 3 courses.

bonus.description: Add `topStudents(int n)` — returns List<Student> of the n students with highest GPA. Sort descending.
Add `courseRoster(String course)` — returns List<String> of student names enrolled in that course.
Add `transferGrades(String fromId, String toId)` — copies all grades from one student to another (e.g., ID change). Validate both students exist.

data_to_use:
Students and courses:
- S001: Aisha — Java(92), Math(88), DSA(95)
- S002: Raj — Java(75), Math(62), DSA(70)
- S003: Priya — Java(85), Math(90), DSA(88)
- S004: Vikram — Java(55), Math(48), DSA(60) ← at risk!
- S005: Anita — Java(88), Math(79), DSA(92)

Passing threshold: 60

Expected:
- Java average: (92+75+85+55+88)/5 = 79.0
- At risk: Vikram (GPA=54.33)
- Java distribution: A=2(Aisha,Anita), B=2(Priya,Raj... wait: Raj=75 is C), let me recalc
  Java: 92(A), 75(C), 85(B), 55(F), 88(B) → A:1, B:2, C:1, F:1
example_output:
=== GRADE BOOK SYSTEM ===

--- Student Lookup ---
Found: Priya (S003), GPA=87.67

--- Java Course Average ---
Java average: 79.0

--- At Risk (below 60) ---
Vikram (S004) GPA=54.33

--- Java Grade Distribution ---
A: 1
B: 2
C: 1
F: 1

=== END ===

criteria: Declares as Map not HashMap (5 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. Key challenges: Student stores grades as Map<String, Integer> (a map INSIDE a map). gradeDistribution must convert numeric grades to letter grades. atRisk must sort by GPA ascending — use a Comparator.
submission_instructions: Save as GradeBookSystem.java. Run it. Push to GitHub — this demonstrates three HashMap patterns in a real educational domain that interviewers recognize.
tips_for_success: Student contains a Map<String, Integer> courseGrades — a map inside the student object.
GradeBook contains Map<String, Student> — key is studentId String.
courseAverage: loop all students in GradeBook.values(), check if student has that course (getGrade != null), sum and count.
gradeDistribution: same loop, convert numeric grade to letter with if-chain.
atRisk: build list, filter by !isPassing(threshold), sort by getGPA() ascending.
concepts_reinforced: HashMap as primary storage (O(1) student lookup)
